topic: An employee is asked to train their replacement
raw_output:
[story 1]

The air in the conference room felt thick, suffocating. Sarah watched Mark, the new hire, nervously fiddle with the strap of his briefcase. He looked so eager, so open, and it just grated on her nerves. "So, Mark," her manager began, a bright, overly cheerful tone that made Sarah’s jaw tighten, "I’d like you to shadow me closely for the next month. I’ll be your guide." Sarah felt a familiar, hot clench in her chest. She forced a smile that felt brittle on her face. Inside, she cataloged every tedious step she would have to repeat, every clumsy mistake she would have to patiently correct. She wanted to see him stumble, to watch his confidence erode under her watchful, superior gaze. She nodded, the movement stiff, the silence stretching out like drawn-out taffy.

[story 2]

"Take the lead on onboarding, David," the director announced, clapping his hands together with an almost alarming enthusiasm. David blinked slowly, trying to process the sudden shift in responsibility. A small, sour feeling bubbled up in his gut. He’d spent years mastering these processes, and now he was relegated to being a glorified babysitter. He met the director’s eyes, and instead of deference, a cool, calculating assessment settled over him. *Let’s see how smoothly this goes,* he thought, a faint smirk playing on his lips before he offered a polite, measured agreement. He began mentally drafting scenarios where he could subtly introduce complications, just to see if the new employee could handle the unexpected friction.

[story 3]

I stood by the water cooler, arms crossed tightly over my chest, pretending to examine a stain on my sleeve. When Brenda announced that I was to mentor the intern, my internal reaction was pure, simmering resentment. I could feel the tension coiling in my shoulders, a hard knot that refused to loosen. The intern, bless his naive heart, looked up at me with wide, trusting eyes. I offered a smile that didn't quite reach my eyes—a thin, practiced curve of the mouth. "Don't worry," I said, my voice deliberately low and smooth, dripping with an exaggerated patience. I made sure my tone conveyed that this was an imposition, a burden I was enduring purely for the sake of watching someone else struggle. Every instruction I gave was calibrated to be just slightly too complex, just enough to ensure the required level of bewilderment.
